content:
# Revelation Chapter 15 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (a), 4th century CE, pages 288-289 | **Source Image:** REV_015_page_001.jpg, REV_015_page_002.jpg

1. And I saw another sign in heaven, great and marvelous: seven angels having seven plagues, the last ones, because in them the wrath of God is completed.

2. And I saw something like a sea of glass mingled with fire, and those who had overcome the beast and his image and the number of his name, standing upon the sea of glass, having harps of God.

3. And they sing the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb, saying, "Great and marvelous are your works, Lord God Almighty; righteous and true are your ways, O King of the nations.

4. Who shall not fear you, O Lord, and glorify your name? For you alone are holy; for all the nations shall come and worship before you, for your righteous acts have been made manifest."

5. And after these things I looked, and the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened,

6. and the seven angels who had the seven plagues came out of the temple, clothed in pure bright linen, and girded around their chests with golden sashes.

7. And one of the four living creatures gave to the seven angels seven golden bowls full of the wrath of God who lives forever and ever.

8. And the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God and from his power, and no one was able to enter the temple until the seven plagues of the seven angels were completed.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

- **15:1 — "the last ones" (tas eschatas)**: ΤΑΣ ΕΣΧΑΤΑΣ — these seven plagues are explicitly identified as the final series of divine judgments, bringing God's wrath to its completion (ΕΤΕΛΕΣΘΗ, from teleō, "to bring to an end/fulfill").
- **15:2 — "the number of his name"**: Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΝ ΑΡΙΘΜΟΝ ΤΟΥ ΟΝΟΜΑΤΟΣ ΑΥΤΟΥ — the victors have overcome not only the beast and his image but also "the number of his name," linking back to the calculation of 13:18. Some manuscripts omit this phrase.
- **15:3 — "the song of Moses...and the song of the Lamb"**: Whether this is one song or two is debated. The single song that follows blends Exodus 15 (the Song of the Sea) with Christological content. Rendered as a hendiadys — one song viewed from two perspectives: deliverance from earthly tyranny (Moses) and eschatological redemption (the Lamb).
- **15:3 — "King of the nations"**: ΒΑΣΙΛΕΥ ΤΩΝ ΕΘΝΩΝ — Sinaiticus reads "nations" (ethnōn). Other manuscripts read "ages" (aiōnōn). The Sinaiticus reading is followed, which echoes Jeremiah 10:7 (LXX): "Who would not fear you, O King of the nations?"
- **15:4 — "you alone are holy"**: ΜΟΝΟΣ ΟΣΙΟΣ — hosios ("holy/devout") rather than hagios ("sacred/set apart"). The term hosios emphasizes moral purity and covenant faithfulness, often applied to God's righteous character in the Psalms (LXX).
- **15:5 — "the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony"**: Ο ΝΑΟΣ ΤΗΣ ΣΚΗΝΗΣ ΤΟΥ ΜΑΡΤΥΡΙΟΥ — a dense phrase combining "temple" (naos), "tabernacle" (skēnē), and "testimony" (martyrion). This echoes the Septuagint's description of the wilderness tabernacle (Numbers 17:7-8 LXX), placing the heavenly reality behind the earthly type.
- **15:6 — "pure bright linen"**: Sinaiticus reads ΛΙΝΟΝ ΚΑΘΑΡΟΝ ΛΑΜΠΡΟΝ ("clean bright linen"). Some manuscripts read ΛΙΘΟΝ ("stone") instead of ΛΙΝΟΝ ("linen"), likely a scribal error in those witnesses. The Sinaiticus reading of "linen" is contextually appropriate for priestly garments.
- **15:8 — temple filled with smoke**: Echoing Isaiah 6:4 and 1 Kings 8:10-11, where the glory of God fills the temple so that the priests cannot minister. The inaccessibility of the temple signals that intercession is suspended during the outpouring of the final plagues.

— LOGOS
